title: Coil-and-pinch combined bowl in air-dry clay
subtitle: A wider bowl built by pinching a base and adding coil walls
excerpt: A coil-and-pinch bowl combines the two most basic hand-building methods: a pinched base gives the bowl its starting curve, and stacked coils extend the walls to the final height. The combination lets you create a wider, shallower form than pinching alone allows. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Combined coil-and-pinch technique derived from first principles. Standard hand-building method in pottery instruction.

glossaryTerms.definition: A vessel formed by pressing the thumb into a ball of clay and pinching the walls outward between thumb and fingers.
A rope of clay rolled to an even diameter, used to build up the walls of a vessel one layer at a time.
Scratching joining surfaces and applying clay slurry before pressing two pieces of clay together.
The drying stage where clay is firm but still slightly damp, the right point for adding coils to a pinched base.

content.text: A
pinch pot
has a natural upward curve that makes a good bowl base. Adding
coils
extends that curve into a wider, deeper vessel than the hands can pinch from a single ball. The two techniques are compatible because they share the same joining method:
score and slip
. No kiln or wheel is needed.

attrs.title: When to add coils
attrs.body: Add coils once the pinched base has reached leather-hard, stiff enough that it holds its shape when you set it on the table. Coils added to a wet base cause the base to collapse. Two to four hours at room temperature is usually enough for a 200 g base.

content.text: What you need
Making the bowl
Step 1: Pinch the base

Roll 200 g of clay into a smooth ball. Press your thumb straight down into the centre to within 10 mm of the base. Begin pinching between thumb and fingers, rotating the ball a quarter-turn after each pinch. Work from the base of the walls upward, keeping the walls 8 to 10 mm thick throughout. The opening should be 12 to 14 cm across when done. Set on a canvas board and leave uncovered for two to three hours until
leather-hard
Step 2: Roll coils and attach the first ring

Roll coils 15 mm in diameter from the remaining clay. Score the top rim of the pinched base and apply slurry. Press one coil around the rim, joining the ends by pressing and blending the join. Blend the interior of the seam with a wooden rib pressed at a 45° angle. Leave the exterior coil ridge visible for texture or smooth it, decide before adding more coils.
Step 3: Add further coils and shape the curve

Add two or three more coils in the same way, scoring and slipping each join and blending the interior. To flare the bowl outward, position each new coil slightly outside the one below. To bring the walls inward, position each coil slightly inside. A gentle outward flare from the base to the rim gives a classic shallow-bowl profile. Keep all wall sections 8 to 10 mm thick.
Step 4: Finish the rim

Trim the top coil level with the needle tool so the rim is even. Compress the rim edge with a wet finger, smoothing it into a rounded lip. Smooth the interior of the bowl with a damp sponge. Dry bone-dry over two to three days, then seal with PVA diluted 1:1 with water inside and out.
Variations
Leave the exterior coil ridges visible and unsmoothed for a textured appearance that shows the construction.
Press a leaf or stamp into the pinched base before it stiffens, adding decoration to the inside base of the bowl.
Common problems

items.symptom: First coil falls away from the pinched base
items.cause: The base was too dry or the slurry too thin.
items.fix: Score the break surface, apply fresh slurry, and press the coil back firmly. Blend the interior seam immediately.

items.symptom: Walls thin out unevenly around the circumference
items.cause: Pinching applied uneven pressure, taking more clay from some sides than others.
items.fix: Check thickness with the needle tool before adding coils. Press small pads of fresh clay onto the thin interior and blend before the base stiffens.
